Author: Vishal lad
Title: AI Won't Shrink Software Development - It May Multiply It
Text:
Note: This article was written with AI assistance. The ideas, interpretation, and conclusions reflect my own thinking.
AI Won’t Shrink Software Development — It May Multiply It
For a long time, people have said that AI will make software development much more efficient.
- Code will be generated faster.
- Teams will be smaller.
- Productivity will soar.
Many of these claims are based on a simple idea:
If coding gets cheaper and easier, we’ll need to do less of it.
That sounds logical and even unavoidable.
But history and economics suggest something more interesting.
The Efficiency Assumption
Whenever a new technology boosts productivity, people often make the same prediction:
- “We’ll need fewer workers.”
- “Demand for this skill will collapse.”
- “Automation will shrink the industry.”
We heard this with:
- Industrial machinery
- Spreadsheets
- Cloud computing
- Low-code platforms
And now, with AI-driven code generation.
If AI can write code in seconds that used to take hours, shouldn’t we need less coding overall?
Not always.
Enter the Jevons Paradox
In the 1800s, economist William Stanley Jevons noticed a surprising pattern while studying coal use in England:
As steam engines became more efficient, coal usage didn’t fall; it actually increased.
Why?
Because making coal-powered energy more efficient made it cheaper, so more industries started using it.
This idea became known as the Jevons Paradox:
When it gets easier to use something, people often use more of it, not less.
Efficiency doesn’t always conserve.
Sometimes, it actually increases demand.
AI Code Generation as an Efficiency Shock
AI is a big step forward in making software development faster.
It reduces:
- Time required to write code
- Cost of development
- Skill barriers
- Friction of experimentation
Tasks that were once required:
- Days of implementation
- Deep syntax recall
- Extensive boilerplate
Now, these can be drafted, improved, and finished much more quickly.
In many cases, coding now costs less, both in money and in mental effort.
What Happens When Coding Becomes Cheap
This is where our instincts can be wrong.
Lower development costs do not automatically mean:
- Less software
- Fewer applications
- Reduced demand
Instead, we are beginning to see:
- More prototypes
- More internal tools
- More automation projects
- More niche solutions
- More experimentation
When it gets easier to build things, people create more.
Projects previously dismissed as:
- “Too expensive”
- “Too time-consuming”
- “Not worth the effort”
These suddenly become possible.
From Scarcity to Abundance
Traditional software development operated under constraints:
- Developer bandwidth
- Budget limitations
- Long build cycles
- High opportunity cost
AI changes that situation.
When software creation becomes more accessible:
- Teams try more ideas
- Businesses automate more processes
- Startups test more products
- Non-engineers build solutions
Having more options doesn’t mean we do less. It just changes the way we work.
Rethinking “Coding Jobs Will Die”
The popular narrative frames AI as a replacement story:
“AI writes code → developers become obsolete”
But Jevons Paradox hints at a different outcome:
“AI lowers coding cost → software demand expands”
This shift isn’t really about cutting jobs. It’s more about changing the way work gets done.
We may see:
- Fewer hours spent on repetitive coding
- Greater emphasis on system design
- Increased need for validation & testing
- More focus on problem framing
- Growth of AI-orchestrated workflows
The kinds of work people do will change over time.
The Real Bottleneck Moves
When code can be written faster, the shortage doesn’t go away. It just shows up in other places.
New bottlenecks emerge:
- Clear problem definition
- Architectural thinking
- Correctness verification
- Security & governance
- Integration complexity
- Domain understanding
Generating code becomes easier.
What really matters is making sure code is correct, safe, scalable, and useful. That’s what will set teams apart.
The Explosion of “Previously Impossible” Software
Cheap coding enables categories of software that were once impractical:
- Hyper-custom internal tools
- Micro-automation workflows
- Short-lived experimental apps
- Domain-specific utilities
- Personal productivity systems
Software is no longer a huge investment. Now, it’s something flexible that teams can use whenever they need it.
Implications for Developers
The future advantage may not belong to those who type fastest, but to those who:
- Frame problems clearly
- Design resilient systems
- Validate AI outputs rigorously
- Integrate across tools & platforms
- Balance speed with reliability
Coding skills still matter, but people will use them differently.
Implications for Enterprises
For organisations, AI-driven efficiency may lead to:
- More software initiatives, not fewer
- Faster experimentation cycles
- Lower cost of failure
- Continuous modernization
- The focus will shift from what we can’t build to how we manage and oversee software projects.
The big question becomes:
“How do we manage an explosion of software creation?”
Not:
“How do we survive a reduction?”
Efficiency Does Not Remove Demand
The history of technology repeatedly shows:
Efficiency → Lower cost → Expanded usage → Greater demand
AI-assisted development may follow the same trajectory.
In the future, there may be less software work.
It may contain:
There will be more software, made more quickly, by more people, and using new approaches.
Closing Thought
People often talk about AI and coding in terms of jobs being replaced. But the bigger change may be happening somewhere else:
- From scarcity → to abundance
- From limitation → to exploration
- From manual effort → to leveraged creation
When coding becomes cheap, we do not stop creating new things. But we end up creating more than we ever imagined.
